title: About Me
body: I’m Han-yu Wang. You can call me Henry. I was born in Taiwan , grew up in Shanghai . Now I’m studying in Hong Kong . I’m a fourth-year student majoring in Applied Artificial Intelligence at the University of Hong Kong . I also work part-time as a research assistant in the SLR Lab , where my work sits between cognitive science and AI, and I’m a Cloud, Data & AI consulting intern at PwC . My research interests sit where AI meets cognitive science . I’m curious how minds and machines learn and reason, and whether looking closely inside one can help us make sense of the other. For a more compact academic and professional summary, read my CV . 多良 · 太麻里 · 台東 Duoliang · Taimali · Taitung

title: On a More Personal Level
body: I love traveling. I've been amazed by the Sagrada Família in Barcelona, awed by the ancient Acropolis in Athens, inspired by the Statue of Liberty in New York, and enchanted by the Redwoods in Rotorua. Each one stuck with me more than any photo really captures. I keep a small globe of those places here. "Once you have traveled, the voyage never ends, but is played out over and over again in the quietest chambers. The mind can never break off from the journey." - Pat Conroy Scroll to see more I also just like building things. There’s a particular satisfaction in watching a pile of code finally come together into something that works. Have a look at my Early Projects if you're curious. When I’m not working, you’ll often find me by the water. In New Zealand it was Napier’s oceanside; in Europe, the Mediterranean, swimming and sunbathing and not doing much else. In Hong Kong I unwind at Kennedy Town Praya. The sea resets me somehow.

title: Say Hello
body: If something here resonates, or you just feel like getting in touch, I’d genuinely like to hear from you. For anything personal, write to me at whenry6688@gmail.com (mailto:whenry6688@gmail.com); for academic matters, henry.why@connect.hku.hk (mailto:henry.why@connect.hku.hk) reaches me best.

title: My Learning Journey So Far
body: Where I’ve studied so far, and a bit of what I got up to along the way.

title: BASc
body: September 2022 - Present The University of Hong Kong Bachelor of Arts and Sciences in Applied Artificial Intelligence I'm a fourth-year student at the University of Hong Kong (https://www.hku.hk/), majoring in Applied Artificial Intelligence (https://admissions.hku.hk/programmes/undergraduate-programmes/bachelor-of-arts-and-sciences-applied-artificial-intelligence). I have taken a diverse range of courses including Python, C++, Java, Data Structures and Algorithms, Software Engineering, Multivariable Calculus, Linear Algebra, Probability and Statistics, Machine Learning, Deep Learning, and Data Science. I was named to the Dean's Honours List for outstanding academic performance. Beyond my coursework, I served as a Student Representative on the CAES Staff-Student Consultative Committee and am currently a Student Peer Advisor (SPA), offering advice and helping freshmen and sophomores make study and career plans. February 2025 - June 2025 The University of Melbourne Semester Exchange Semester exchange at The University of Melbourne (https://www.unimelb.edu.au/) through HKU Worldwide Student Exchange (https://intlaffairs.hku.hk/hku-worldwide-student-exchange) program. Relevant Coursework: Optimisation, Machine Learning, Principles of Finance

title: IBDP
body: September 2017 - June 2022 Shanghai High School International Division International Baccalaureate I completed my junior high, high school, and International Baccalaureate diplomas at Shanghai High School International Division (https://www.shsid.org/). For my IB diploma, I studied a broad array of subjects including computer science, mathematics, economics, physics, English, and Chinese. In addition to my coursework, I co-founded SHSID Musix Board, a school-wide popular music opinion-sharing platform, and led the Coding Club. July 2019 - August 2019 Havelock North High School Visiting Student I spent five weeks as a visiting student at Havelock North High School (https://www.hnhs.school.nz/) in Hawke's Bay, New Zealand, sitting in classes alongside local students and getting a first-hand feel for everyday Kiwi life. I took courses in English, Calculus, Statistics, Chemistry, Physics, and English as a Second Language, and joined in the wider life of the school.

title: My Work Experience
body: A few of the roles that shaped how I think about technology, and about what it takes to turn an idea into something people actually use.

July 2026 - December 2026 Cloud, Data & AI Consulting Intern PricewaterhouseCoopers, Hong Kong I'm a Cloud, Data & AI Consulting Intern at PwC Hong Kong, a natural next step after my earlier work in AI, data, and digital transformation. What interests me most is seeing how technical ideas play out in consulting settings, especially when they need to be explained clearly and connected to business decisions. The work is sharpening how I think from both sides: the technical side, and the side of the people and organizations actually using the technology.

May 2024 - Present Part-time Student Research Assistant Speech, Language, and Reading Lab, HKU, Hong Kong This role is closely related to a question I have been thinking about for a long time: how do people learn, remember, and gradually change through experience? I work with experimental data and computational models to study learning behaviour over time. What interests me most is not only whether a model performs well, but whether it captures something real about the way people learn. The work has pulled me further into the space between cognitive science and AI, especially questions around memory, structure, and learning processes. August 2024 - December 2024 Digital Transformation Intern Hongkong and Shanghai Banking Corporation Limited, Hong Kong My internship at HSBC was the first time I saw how much of digital transformation is really about structure before it is about technology. I worked on product and distribution mapping across different markets, where the information often came in fragments and rarely in the same shape twice. A surprising amount of the work was simply making things comparable. I was also involved in a UI transformation project. Business fields, scenarios, and UI components all had to be organised into something much clearer than the original mix of documents, spreadsheets, and conversations. Part of my work there became an Excel and VBA-based workflow, which ended up being one of the more satisfying things I built that year. Alongside that, I sat in on a number of AI use case discussions in personal banking. That was probably where I became more cautious, in a good way, about AI. The interesting question was rarely whether something could be done with AI, but whether it made sense in context, given the workflow, the risk, and the people still needed around it. July 2024 - April 2025 Part-time Student Research Assistant Psychometrics and Data Science Lab, HKU, Hong Kong At the PDS Lab, I worked on ideas around AI, education, and personali

June 2023 - December 2023 Web Developer, Digital Content Support Kiwiview International Limited, New Zealand Kiwiview was one of my earliest experiences working in a company setting, and it gave me a first sense of what it means to be responsible for something outside school. I worked on the company website from design to development to hosting, and also helped with branding materials, website content, and promotional posts for study-tour and travel programmes. What stayed with me from that period was the feeling that even fairly small design and web decisions could quietly shape how a business presents itself.

title: Research I've Been Part Of
body: My research sits where cognitive science meets AI. I want to understand the principles behind how a system, human or artificial, learns from experience, reasons, and decides, and I study them from both sides at once. On the human side, I ask how the mind turns experience into structure: how we pick up patterns, when that learning hardens into an explicit rule rather than a feel for the examples, and what a learning curve or a reaction time can actually tell us about the process underneath. I write these processes down as computational models and test them against behavior, some of it as a part-time research assistant at HKU's SLR Lab (https://slrlab.edu.hku.hk/), with Dr. Stephen M. K. Lee. On the machine side, I put the same questions to AI systems, language models in particular, with interpretability as the through-line: not just what a model does, but what it represents and how it computes that inside, turning opaque behavior into mechanisms we can name and reason about. I work through model internals and carefully designed behavioral tests, sometimes putting models and people through the very same task. For me these are not two fields but one question asked twice. The longer game is an account of learning and reasoning precise enough to hold for a person and a model alike, where what we learn about minds helps us read what is going on inside machines, and what we find inside machines feeds back into how we understand ourselves. The projects below are early steps in that direction. Human learning and computational cognition Computational models of how people learn, represent, and generalize. September 2025 – June 2026

title: Projects I've Been Up To
body: These are things I built because I wanted a clearer way to work with an idea, a task, or a habit. Some came out of a lab or a job, others from coursework or my own time. They range from AI tools and learning systems to visual explanations and small automations. Underneath, though, I keep coming back to the same thing: how people think and learn, and how the right tool can make something complicated easy to inspect, use, and play with. Professional and lab work Projects built inside companies and research labs. July 2024 - December 2024

title: Insurance Product Configuration Engine
body: An engine that turns an insurance product into the exact app screens it needs Associated with HSBC (https://www.hsbc.com/) On HSBC's insurance digital-transformation team, I tackled a quietly painful problem: across dozens of insurance products and a dozen markets, which app screens should each product actually show? Those rules lived scattered across business, design, and engineering, and kept drifting out of sync. I encoded them into one structure: three linked libraries plus a decision table, driven by an Excel and VBA engine that turns a product's specification into the exact set of UI components it needs, automatically. It went through four versions and shipped with a built-in tutorial so the team could keep running it after I left. Because the real logic is dense, I rebuilt a small, playable version you can drive in your browser, with a walkthrough that explains the problem before it hands you the controls: Try the interactive walkthrough → July 2024 - April 2025

title: Advancing Generative AI in Personalized Learning
body: A Full-Stack Adaptive Learning Portal with Vue, Flask, and Firestore Associated with PDS Lab (https://psym-ds.github.io/) Working with Prof. Jinsong Chen, I built an AI-powered education portal from scratch with Vue.js, Flask, and Firestore. It has separate views for learners, instructors, and admins: you can build question banks, design assessment templates, and let the system suggest questions and resources based on how each student is doing. Assigning content to a person or a group takes a few clicks, and the analytics are there to show instructors where someone is stuck, so they know when to step in. Check out the live demo: LLMAS Portal (https://llmas.pds-lab.org/) Coursework University course projects, taken further than the brief asked. November 2025 – December 2025

title: Interactive Scrollytelling Deck
body: A Schema-Driven Scrollytelling Engine with a Pluggable D3 Chart Library This started as a course project: I had a data-heavy topic to present and wanted to tell it as a story that unfolds while you scroll, not a wall of charts. It grew well past the assignment into a reusable scrollytelling engine. Every scene is declared in a single schema, so text, tables, cards, and charts share consistent layout and reveal timing, with a registry mapping each figure to a D3 chart builder: line, ridge, scatter, timeline, heatmap, geo maps, Sankey, and chord. A background manager streams a different looping video per section, and it all runs as plain ES modules with no build step, so it hosts anywhere static. The live demo below is the course deck itself, built entirely on the engine. Walkthrough: See how the engine works → Live demo: henryw.me/CCGL9074 (https://www.henryw.me/CCGL9074/) September 2025 – December 2025

title: Stride Support
body: Customer Support Copilot with MCP Tools and Local LLM Stride Support is a customer support copilot that runs entirely on-device. A FastAPI router speaks the OpenAI-compatible API and orchestrates five MCP (Model Context Protocol) tool servers (catalog search, order lookups, complaints, recommendations, and policy QA) over a local LLM via LM Studio. The React UI lets the user pick from ongoing or past orders, and the router injects that context into every prompt so the model stays grounded in what the customer actually has. A semantic similarity gate blocks off-topic queries, and a fine-tuned mBERT classifier tags sentiment in real time so the model can adjust tone. Project page: github.com/backprop07/STAT4011 (https://github.com/backprop07/STAT4011) On my own time Side projects, built outside work and class. February 2026 – Present

title: Congentra
body: AI-Native Oral Verification Platform for Assignment Understanding I'm building Congentra, a platform that helps teachers check whether a student actually understands the work they handed in and can defend it. Instead of trying to detect AI use, it reads the assignment and the submission, generates a few targeted oral questions, collects the student's spoken answers on their own time, and packages everything into one evidence file a reviewer can skim. The whole thing is built around the reviewer: less time spent, and more to actually go on when making a call. February 2025 - June 2025

title: Lexaday
body: A Daily Vocabulary Coach with Generative Mnemonics and Spaced Review Lexaday is a small learning system that turns word roots into a daily email habit. Each run chooses a new root, generates a short motivational hook, creates example words and sentences, attaches a visual mnemonic, and brings back older roots on a growing review interval. The project keeps per-user progress in JSON, commits generated images and state through GitHub Actions, and uses Hugging Face models for both language and image generation so the email feels more like a personalized lesson than a static flashcard. Walkthrough: See how Lexaday works → Project repo: github.com/henryhyw/lexaday (https://github.com/henryhyw/lexaday) February 2025 - June 2025

title: Dealtracker
body: A Personal Deal Hunter with Filters and Email Digests Dealtracker is a practical shopping agent: it watches sale pages, applies my own brand, gender, size, and discount thresholds, normalizes messy brand names across stores, deduplicates products against a local history file, and only sends an email when something genuinely new clears the bar. The useful part is the last mile: instead of a raw scrape, the output is a clean Gmail digest grouped by brand, with product images, original and sale prices, discount percentages, and direct links ready to act on. Walkthrough: See how Dealtracker works → Project repo: github.com/henryhyw/dealtracker (https://github.com/henryhyw/dealtracker) September 2017 - June 2022

title: My Early Projects in Secondary School
body: Downloaders, Browser Extensions, and an Economics Graph Generator During my secondary school years, I got to work on a bunch of projects that really sparked my love for coding. A few that stand out: a batch downloader for Scientific American episodes, a couple of Chrome extensions, the website for Kiwiview International Limited, and an economics graph generator called Econographer. They're a decent record of how excited I was, and still am, about programming and figuring problems out. For more details, please visit: My Early CS Adventures .

title: Insurance Product Configuration Engine
body: Here's something you've done a hundred times: packing for a trip . The kind of trip decides what goes in the bag. Say "beach" and you need swimwear; add "water sports" and a snorkel earns its place; say "ski" and it's a completely different bag. You're running a tiny rules engine in your head, turning a few answers into the right list. At HSBC I built exactly that engine, for a much harder version of the same problem. Below is a small, fully playable version. It takes a couple of minutes, and the second half hands you the controls.

title: First, the real problem
body: Picture one banking app that sells many kinds of insurance. Every product needs a different set of screens : a travel policy asks for trip dates, a car policy asks about your vehicle, and some questions only appear once an earlier answer unlocks them. Now multiply that by dozens of products across a dozen markets. The rules for "which screen shows when" lived scattered in people's heads. Business knew the requirements, designers knew the screens, developers wired it together, and over time the three drifted out of sync. There was no single place you could point to and say this is how a product becomes a set of screens.

title: The idea: stop mixing three questions
body: I split the mess into three small libraries, each owned by the team that actually knows the answer: Spec What does the business need to know? The questions. Components What has the team actually built? The building blocks. Config Small rule tables linking the two: given these answers, use these blocks. Because they're separate, business can add a question, design can add a block, and neither breaks the other. Those rule tables are where the magic happens, and they're what you'll drive next.

title: Now you try it
body: Same machine, on the friendly example: you're packing for a trip. Answer the questions, watch each little rule table pick a winner, then watch the bag fill with exactly the right things. 1 Tell us about the trip ↳ Notice the follow-up question appears only when it's relevant. In the real tool that condition is written right into the field's name, so the form reshapes itself as you fill it in. 2 Each decision gets its own rule table Every table works the same way: keep the columns whose conditions all match your answers, and the most specific one on the right wins. ALL is a wildcard that matches anything. Your answers (inputs) What to pack (outputs) 1"> Your answers match columns; the most specific, “ ” , wins. Your answers land on “ ” . 3 The bag packs itself Packing list Just the essentials for this trip. Same answers in, the same list out, every time. Each table contributes its part, and together they build the bag. Nobody has to remember the rules, because the tables do.

title: What this really was
body: The real version ran in Excel, driven by a roughly 32 KB VBA engine. It had a small custom language for those conditional follow-up questions, a stack of decision tables with wildcards and right-side overrides (exactly what you just used), and live form behaviour so non-coders could fill it in without breaking anything. It went through four versions and shipped with a built-in tutorial so the next person could take it over. The packing example here stands in for confidential product data, but the machinery is the real thing: a way to turn scattered, tribal business logic into one structure that a team, and a computer, can both read. ← Back to projects

title: Lexaday
body: Lexaday rests on a few ideas I find convincing. Learning a word's root is leverage: one root carries a whole family of words, so a single lesson stretches far. Spacing the reviews along the forgetting curve means a word resurfaces just as it starts to fade, which is when the reminder actually does something. And generative AI does the legwork, fleshing out each root with example sentences and a mnemonic image instead of a bare definition. It all comes as one small email a day.

title: A small lesson, generated fresh each morning
body: Instead of a static flashcard, Lexaday builds a whole email around one root: what it means, the real words it shows up in, a sentence for each, and an image to anchor the meaning.

title: The learning loop
body: It comes down to roots and spacing. Learn "aud" (to hear) and audible, audition and auditorium come almost for free. And rather than drilling a root every day, Lexaday widens the gap each time it returns, one run, then two, four, eight, so a review lands just as the word starts to fade. Everyone has their own progress file, so the timing fits how much they have already seen. 1

title: Pick
body: Choose one new root the user has not seen yet, while scanning past roots for scheduled review. 2

title: Write
body: Generate a short motivational hook and clear example sentences for the words built from that root. 3

title: Imagine
body: Turn the root meaning into a visual prompt, then create a mnemonic image for the email. 4

title: Send
body: Build an HTML email, attach the generated images inline, save the new state, and repeat tomorrow.

title: What the reader receives
body: The email is plain on purpose: open Gmail, read one root, look at the picture, skim a few examples, and revisit a couple of older roots. The work that produced it stays out of sight, so the daily habit costs little. A generated email pairs the root with its meaning, a visual mnemonic, example words, and a review section when older roots are due.

title: How it is packaged
body: Personal memory Each user has a lightweight JSON record for run count, first introduction, last review, and review interval. Generated content Language generation fills in quotes and examples; image generation produces mnemonic artwork for the root. Daily automation A scheduled GitHub Actions run refreshes lessons, images, and state without needing a separate app interface. Project repo: github.com/henryhyw/lexaday (https://github.com/henryhyw/lexaday) ← Back to projects

title: Dealtracker
body: I spent a semester on exchange in Australia, and the outdoor gear I'd wanted back home, Arc'teryx, Patagonia and the like, turned out to be both cheaper than in Hong Kong or the mainland and almost always discounted somewhere. The catch was timing: a sale would surface and vanish, lost in pages and pages of products. Dealtracker is what I built so I'd stop missing them. It watches the shops for me and emails me the moment something I actually want drops to a price I'd actually pay.

title: From noisy sale pages to a short inbox digest
body: The idea is simple: pick the stores, spell out exactly what I want, brand, size, gender, minimum discount, then let a scheduled job read the sale pages for me and surface only the handful of matches worth opening. 2 Australian stores 7 brands I track 29-39% minimum discount

title: The email output
body: The output is the email itself: matching products grouped by brand and sorted by discount, each with a photo, the original and sale price, the percentage off, and a link straight to the product. Arc'teryx and The North Face digest Patagonia and Icebreaker digest

title: What happens behind the email
body: It does one narrow job: check these shops on a schedule, and email me only when something I'd actually buy has gone on sale. To do that it scrapes two Australian outdoor retailers, WildEarth and Find Your Feet. Search Build store-specific sale URLs from saved filters for brands, sizes, gender, and minimum discount. Read Open the pages in a headless browser, including dynamic product grids that need time to load. Clean Normalize brand names such as Arc'teryx versus Arcteryx and detect product gender from titles. Compare Check each result against a saved history file so the email contains new deals, not repeated noise. Notify Send a compact HTML digest only when something new passes the filters.

title: The control layer
body: Dealtracker also keeps half an eye on the inbox. Emailing it a one-word reset clears the saved history, which is handy when I want to start the baseline fresh without touching the script or the data file. Specific filters Each store has its own URL pattern, so filters are encoded in the way that store actually expects. Human-friendly output The email is built to be acted on: brand sections, product cards, prices, and links. Low-maintenance memory A local history file keeps past product links and names, preventing the same sale from being announced repeatedly. Project repo: github.com/henryhyw/dealtracker (https://github.com/henryhyw/dealtracker) ← Back to projects

title: Scrollytelling Engine
body: A course left me with a data-heavy topic to present: maps, distributions, and flows that only land once you can actually see them. Static slides could not carry it. I wanted the data to animate, and the argument to unfold as you read rather than all at once, so I built it as a web page instead, a story you move through by scrolling. Partway in, the machinery turned out to be general, so I pulled it into a small engine I can point at any subject. The deck at the bottom is one thing made with it.

title: A scene is just data
body: Here is the idea, running live. Every scene below is drawn by the engine's own code, the same builders the real deck uses. You describe a figure by its type and a few props, a registry hands it to the matching builder, and it animates itself in. Step through them. Add a scene type and it is one entry in the registry; use it and it is a few lines of data. You never touch render code.

title: See it as a story
body: The pieces above are the vocabulary. Below is the deck they were built for, running in full and embedded right here. Scroll inside it: the visuals hold while the words and data move past, which is the whole point. Cramped in the frame? Open it fullscreen ↗ (https://www.henryw.me/CCGL9074/)

title: Under the hood
body: Declarative slides Each scene is one object: its background group, its nav label, and the figures it holds. Layout, typography, and reveal timing come from shared tokens. A figure registry The type on a figure is looked up in a registry of builders, covering text, line, ridge, scatter, heatmap, geo maps, Sankey, chord, and more. Background manager Looping video layers stream per section group, so each part of the story carries its own mood without any extra markup. Zero build Plain ES modules with D3, d3-sankey, and TopoJSON loaded over CDN. It runs from any static host with nothing to compile. Live demo: henryw.me/CCGL9074 (https://www.henryw.me/CCGL9074/) Project repo: github.com/henryhyw/CCGL9074 (https://github.com/henryhyw/CCGL9074) ← Back to projects

title: My Early Adventures in Computer Science
body: Here are the adventures I went through during my junior high and high school years. I've highlighted some main projects from each year. I'll admit upfront: a lot of what I did back then was pretty naive. But who hasn't had a naive phase, right? Honestly, I might still be a bit naive now, and that's okay.

title: How It All Started
body: I fell in love with computer science when I was 13, in my first year of junior high school. My computer science teacher demonstrated how a batch script can create folders automatically. From that moment, I was fascinated by how computers could be programmed to do all sorts of cool things. I taught myself the basics of Java and Python and started building simple apps, like a contact notebook app in Java and an advanced number guessing game with user accounts and game history logged in text files. Here are some screenshots of this first app (contact notebook) I made.

title: Batch Downloading Scientific American Episodes
body: In my first year of junior high, I decided to practice English and discovered the podcast called Scientific American. At that time, I was using an MP3 player that supported playing audio, images, and texts. So, I developed an app to batch download audios, scripts, and images from Scientific American episodes. The app included a user account system that could record the episodes a user had downloaded and continue from where they left off next time. Here are some screenshots of this app.

title: Chrome Extension Experiments
body: In my second year of junior high, I started exploring Chrome extensions. Living in Mainland China made me curious about things that weren't easily accessible, so I'd use a VPN to access Google-related stuff. It amazed me that these small apps in the browser could do so much, like turning the screen dark for YouTube videos. So, I learned how to develop and publish Chrome extensions and created a few: Easy QR Code : Displays QR codes for the current website, makes your own QR codes, and converts text to QR codes. Page Muter : Mutes the audio or video of a specific tab. Image Fetcher : Batch downloads images from the current website. SA Fetcher : Batch downloads audios, scripts, and images from Scientific American episodes. This is a simplified version of the previous SA Fetcher app. WeChat Spamming : Automatically sends various kinds of spam messages on WeChat (I made this to prank my friends, and surprisingly, it even has 167 users according to the developer dashboard). Here are some screenshots of the developer dashboard and some of the extensions.

title: Music Streaming Hacks
body: In my first year of high school, my main interest was building an app for downloading paid songs from music streaming services in China. I now realize that this was illegal, but at the time, I wasn't aware of that. Although I'm not proud of it, I still want to mention it because it was a masterpiece for a first-year high school student. I did a lot of research on web scraping and managed to crack the links of paid songs from various music streaming services. With this app, users could download songs with the link of the song page from QQ Music, Netease Music, KuGou Music, and KuWo Music, with the desired sound quality. Users could also batch download songs from a singer with a specified number of pages. I even created a user account system where some functions, like downloading lossless audio, were only available to VIP users. It took me several months to develop this app, but it only lasted for half a year because I later realized it wasn't quite legal, so I shut it down. Despite that, it remains one of my fondest memories. By that time, it had 413 users, with 228 QQ Music playlist downloads, 1294 QQ Music single downloads, 523 NetEase Music single downloads, 23 KuGou Music single downloads, and 47 KuWo Music single downloads. Here are some screenshots of the app, which is in Simplified Chinese since the users were from Mainland China.

title: Building Kiwiview's Website
body: In my second year of high school, I started building a website for Kiwiview International Limited, a New Zealand company co-owned by my parents and a Kiwi. Since the business was just starting, they let me try building the website. I taught myself HTML, JavaScript, CSS, and SQL. It took me months to deploy it on an IIS server on Tencent Cloud. The deployment process was really complicated for a beginner, and I spent a lot of time researching it.

title: Simple Automation
body: In my third year of high school, I came across a mental arithmetic and "24-point" game event. It was an online game event held at our school. I created a script to automatically solve the problems, achieving really high scores. The script recognized the content on the screen, used algorithms to solve the problems, and then instructed the computer to input the answers. Everything was done automatically and repeatedly until time was up. Here are some videos of the script I recorded. Your browser does not support the video tag. Your browser does not support the video tag.

title: Econographer, an Economics Graphs Generator
body: In my third year of high school, I started to wonder if the graphs we needed for our economics class could be generated by software. After searching online and not finding anything that met my expectations, I decided to create my own solution. I developed an Office Word add-in called "Econographer," specifically designed for generating the various types of economics graphs used in AP and IB courses. These graphs are highly customizable to fit any requirement. Here's a full demonstration video, recorded by me, showing how it works. Your browser does not support the video tag. I hope you enjoyed reading about my early journey with computer science! ← Back to projects

title: Fragments
body: Loose fragments of thought and life. Fragment entries should be used only when the user asks about a clearly related topic.

title: Expression, Recording, and the Compressed Version of Thought
body: Expression preserves a thought by turning it into language, but the same act also compresses and reshapes it.

title: Expression, Recording, and the Compressed Version of Thought (2)
body:
When a thought is still in my head, it is usually not a sentence. It is more like a mass of feeling, direction, memory, and connections that are not yet fully separated. Language cuts that mass into words, order, and boundaries. That makes the thought easier to hold, share, revise, and return to later, but it also means the thought has already been compressed.

Expression is useful because without it many thoughts disappear. Once something is said or written, it becomes something that can be handled. Other people can understand it, challenge it, and I can look at it again. It can be saved, remembered, organized further, and placed into the next round of thinking.

But expression is not free. The expressed version is not the original mass. It preserves some structure and makes the thought transmissible, but the parts that did not enter language are compressed away. The harder part is that later thinking often begins from the sentence I just produced, not from the original thing that produced it.

That sentence becomes the new starting point. I may continue explaining it, adding to it, modifying it, or defending it. Once a thought becomes language, it feels like an object I can hold, but precisely because I can hold it, it also starts to grow according to the shape of language.

Different languages may work this way too. The point is not that one language is more rational than another, but that different languages may cut experience differently. If the cut changes, the later path of thought may also change. Translation is not only transfer; it is another cut through the original mass.

AI fits here as an organizer of already-expressed material. It can summarize, classify, recombine, and find connections among paragraphs and notes. But what it receives is already the version I have said. It does not directly access the experience, hesitation, bodily state, and intuition that produced the thought before language cut it apart.

So AI is useful, but its place is closer to

title: Labels, Goals, and Rules as Ways of Shaping People
body: Labels, goals, and rules make a complex person readable, expected, and constrained, then shape that person in return.

title: Labels, Goals, and Rules as Ways of Shaping People (2)
body:
If thoughts can be compressed by language, then people can be compressed by labels. A person is a complicated mass of experiences, behaviors, habits, desires, fears, interests, abilities, and tendencies. These things are continuous and do not naturally come in neat blocks, but other people cannot read a full internal structure directly, so we hand over compressed versions of ourselves.

Labels are useful. Personality types, professional identities, research directions, personal profiles, and other people’s descriptions all help make a person readable. A label is like a handle: it lets others hold onto part of you, and it lets you hold onto part of yourself. But the handle is not the person.

The problem is that labels do not stay outside. A label may begin as a summary, but after being repeated, it can begin to shape future behavior. You may start to live up to it, defend it, perform it, or ignore parts of yourself that do not fit. A sentence that first described a person can start helping produce that person.

External labels also work like training signals. Other people compress behavior into judgments, then treat you through those judgments. Those labels change expectations, opportunities, feedback, and limits. Over time, you may begin to predict yourself through the same compressed version, and the internal structure can also shift.

Goals work similarly, except that they are often self-imposed. A goal compresses the future into a discrete result and pulls behavior toward it. It gives continuous behavior direction, but it can also make a person compress themselves into the shape needed by that goal.

Rules add another layer. When someone’s internal structure has not naturally produced the output that society expects, law, morality, etiquette, institutions, school requirements, or family expectations may constrain behavior first. A rule says what cannot be said, what must be done, or which first reaction should not become final output.

Very often, rules are not

title: Labels, Goals, and Rules as Ways of Shaping People (3)
body: This is why being summarized can feel subtly uncomfortable. A summary creates a version of you that is easier to circulate. That version may be clear and convenient, but it may not preserve the core that matters. The issue is not only whether labels, goals, and rules are accurate, but that they return to the person and shape the person who has been compressed.

title: Memory Compresses Time, but the Self Is Changed by Passing Through It
body: Memory turns continuous life into stages and stories, but the self is shaped by actually moving through time.

title: Memory Compresses Time, but the Self Is Changed by Passing Through It (2)
body:
Life, while it is happening, does not really come in sections. It does not announce that this part is high school, this part is university, and this year is a turning point. Real time feels more like something that keeps flowing forward. Many changes have no clear boundary while they are happening.

Memory is not good at preserving that continuity. The closer something is, the more detail it has. Once time moves further away, many things get compressed. A year becomes a few keywords, a place becomes a feeling, a relationship becomes a few images. Distant life is not actually made only of keywords; it is stored by memory at a higher compression rate.

So life stages may not be the structure of life itself, but a structure that memory later adds. This is useful because the past is too large to hold in full detail. But compression loses ordinary afternoons, small jokes, boredom, repetition, and many details that did not survive the later summary.

Old photos, chat records, and notes can feel strange because they reopen the messier version of a period that memory had simplified. The further away time is, the more memory starts to look like labels. We need those labels, but they are not the past itself.

This also changes how I think about the self. I do not really like saying that the future self is simply another person, because that cuts the continuous line between versions. A person is not a fixed point. A person is more like a trajectory that keeps changing.

The future me is not a stranger who suddenly appears in the distance. He is the state of this current system after many updates. Each step may be small, but many small changes together can become a clearly different person. What makes both versions still me may not be similarity, but the continuous path between them.

This makes the question of memory and experience stranger. If someone could jump over a difficult period while keeping the memory and the feeling of continuity, would anything be missing? My first

title: Satisfaction Is Not a Fixed Reward
body: Satisfaction is better understood as a changing signal generated through behavior, time, individual difference, and interpretation.

title: Satisfaction Is Not a Fixed Reward (2)
body:
I sometimes wonder whether happiness and satisfaction can be treated as the same thing. This is debatable, but I tend to first understand happiness as a broad kind of satisfaction. The important point is that satisfaction cannot only mean immediate pleasure.

Eating something good, delaying gratification, finishing a goal, controlling an impulse, helping someone, buying something at a good price, or feeling that I made a more rational choice can all produce satisfaction. Delayed gratification is not necessarily the opposite of satisfaction. It may be another satisfaction curve.

I think of satisfaction as an internal feedback signal. Different activities trigger different signals, and different people receive them with different strengths, durations, delays, and shapes. The same activity may be enjoyable to one person and meaningless to another, or enjoyable to the same person only under a certain interpretation.

This is why rationality cannot always be judged from the outside. Buying discounted products is a good example. For one person, searching for discounts is a waste of time. For another, it produces a small win: the search, the comparison, the discovery, the feeling of being good at living, and the sense that resources are under control.

Opportunity cost should not only count money and time. More accurately, it is the satisfaction you give up from another possible activity. If someone enjoys the search, the time spent is not pure cost. If someone finds it draining, then it is closer to cost.

Satisfaction is not something the outside world simply gives to a person. Often, the behavior itself is only the material. The actual satisfaction is generated through a person’s internal structure, interpretation, goal system, and the unfolding of time.

This connects to labels and language. If the same activity is interpreted as wasting time, it may produce irritation. If it is interpreted as completing a small optimization, it may produce satisfaction. This does not

title: Oysters and Black Truffle Toast at Queen Victoria Market
body: A Melbourne food memory from my last day before leaving exchange: oysters with lemon juice and black truffle toast at Queen Victoria Market.

title: Oysters and Black Truffle Toast at Queen Victoria Market (2)
body:
Queen Victoria Market in Melbourne gave me one of those food memories I will keep thinking about after leaving the city.

The oysters were fresh, cold, and clean, and they were really good with lemon juice. That sharpness made the oyster taste even clearer. The black truffle toast was also unexpectedly satisfying: warm, rich, fragrant, and direct.

What makes it especially memorable is that I went there with my sister on my last day before leaving Melbourne exchange. It was not just about the food, but also about the timing: one last market morning, one last meal before leaving, and something simple that became tied to the whole exchange period.

This is one of the foods I will keep missing after leaving Melbourne.

title: Chaoshan Beef Hot Pot in Guangzhou
body: A Guangzhou food memory with my father: Chaoshan beef hot pot, especially beef meatball and diaolong, eaten without sauce for the clean beef flavor.

title: Chaoshan Beef Hot Pot in Guangzhou (2)
body:
When I travelled to Guangzhou with my father, we went to eat Chaoshan beef hot pot together. That made the meal memorable to me, not only because the food was good, but because it became part of that trip with him.

I really like Chaoshan-style beef. I especially like the beef meatball and diaolong. The meatball has that springy texture and deep beef taste, and diaolong has a clean, tender freshness that works very well in hot pot.

I also tend not to dip it in sauce. The plain beef flavor is already good enough. I like the original, fresh taste of the beef itself, without covering it up too much.

title: AI-Generated Kitten Videos and Manufactured Cuteness
body: AI-generated kitten videos are a good example of synthetic content still reaching human senses and triggering real emotional responses.

title: AI-Generated Kitten Videos and Manufactured Cuteness (2)
body:
I have seen many AI-generated videos on social media where kittens interact with humans, and also some more surreal ones, like a plush watermelon being opened and turning out to contain little kittens. I find them very cute, and clearly many other people like them too.

What interests me is not only that the videos are cute. They are a good example of generative AI content triggering human emotion. The kittens are not real in the usual sense, but the content still reaches human perception: softness, smallness, eye contact, movement, care, surprise. These signals can still produce emotional fluctuation.

That makes this kind of AI-generated content valuable. It can create things that touch human senses and emotions, even when the objects are synthetic. But it also needs caution, because the same ability can be abused. If generated content can reliably press emotional buttons, then it can also be used to manipulate attention, sympathy, desire, or belief.

So I do not think the value of generative AI content is only whether it is realistic. Sometimes its value is that it can generate emotionally meaningful sensory patterns. But precisely because those patterns work on people, they need to be treated carefully.

title: A Conversation About Identity
body: After taking part in an interview about growing up between Shanghai and Taiwanese family roots, I found a clearer way to describe identity: not confused, but layered.

title: A Conversation About Identity (2)
body:
I once took part in an interview about identity and growing up with a Taiwanese family background while living mostly in Shanghai. It was not something I had planned to write publicly at first, but the conversation helped me organize many scattered feelings that had existed in the background for a long time.

What stayed with me most was not a dramatic conclusion, but a quieter one: identity is not always a single answer. It can be made of several layers: where I grew up, where my family comes from, what languages and habits shaped me, what places feel familiar, and how I introduce myself in different contexts.

The interview helped me see that this is not necessarily confusion. It is more like a layered structure. Different parts become more visible in different situations. Sometimes Shanghai feels like the most natural answer. Sometimes Taiwanese family roots matter more. Neither layer fully cancels the other.

That is why the conversation was meaningful to me. It gave me a neutral way to look back at my background without forcing it into one simple label.

title: Han-yu Wang
body: Applied AI undergraduate, University of Hong Kong whenry6688@gmail.com (mailto:whenry6688@gmail.com) · henryw.me (https://www.henryw.me/) · LinkedIn (https://www.linkedin.com/in/henrywhy/) · GitHub (https://github.com/henryhyw/)

title: Profile
body: Fourth-year Applied Artificial Intelligence student at the University of Hong Kong, working where cognitive science meets AI. My research runs along two lines: how people learn and remember, and what goes on inside language models as they learn and reason, studied through behavioral experiments, model internals, and computational modeling. Part-time research assistant at HKU's SLR Lab and Cloud, Data & AI consulting intern at PwC.
